title: REPUBLIC OF KENYA
text:
REPUBLIC OF KENYA
IN THE TAX APPEALS TRIBUNAL AT NAIROBI
TAX APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025
SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED .………………....
APPELLANT
-VS-
COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION…….
RESPONDENT
JUDGEMENT
BACKGROUND
1. The Appellant is a private limited company carrying on the
business of retail sale of liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) and other
products.
2. The Respondent is a principal officer appointed under Section 13
of the Kenya Revenue Authority Act, CAP 469 of Kenya’s Laws
(hereinafter “the Act”). Under Section 5 (1) of the Act, the Kenya
Revenue Authority is an agency of the Government for the
collection and receipt of all tax revenue. Further, under Section
5(2) of the Act with respect to the performance of its functions
under subsection (1), the Authority is mandated to administer
and enforce all provisions of the written laws as set out in Part 1
and 2 of the First Schedule to the Act for the purposes of
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 1 OF 33

assessing, collecting and accounting for all revenues in
accordance with those laws.
3. The Respondent obtained and analyzed the Appellant’s bank
statements together with purchases claimed in order to
establish expected income, compared the same with the i-Tax
declarations, and subjected the variances to both Income Tax
and VAT, resulting in a total principal tax liability of Kshs.
17,752,866.00, comprising corporation tax of Kshs.
1,858,132.00 and VAT of Kshs. 15,894,734.00 after the
Appellant was reported by customers with claims of underfilling
gas.
4. The Respondent served the Appellant with a notice of intention
to audit dated 16th October 2024 thereafter issued a notice of
findings and intention to issue additional assessments for the
period 2019 to 2023 on 4th December 2024 and issued
additional assessments on 16th December 2024.
5. The Appellant objected to the said assessments on 10th January
2025, clearly setting out the grounds of objection that the
assessments.
6. The Respondent however confirmed the assessments through
an objection decision dated 10th March 2025, which decision
forms the subject of this Appeal.
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 2 OF 33

7. Dissatisfied with the Respondent’s tax decision on 10th March,
2025, the Appellant filed a notice of appeal dated and filed 7th
April 2025.
THE APPEAL
8. The Appellant lodged its Memorandum of appeal filed on 21st
April, 2025 raising the following grounds of appeal:
(i) The assessments are erroneous, irrational and excessive;
(ii) The Respondent subjected non-existent income to VAT and
Income Tax using a banking test without doing a full and
proper reconciliation of non-business income;
(iii) The Respondent did not allow the whole expenditure
incurred in earning income contrary to Section 15 of the
Income Tax Act, CAP 470 of the Laws of Kenya (hereinafter
“ITA”);
(iv) The Respondent disallowed genuinely incurred input VAT
contrary to section 17 of the Value Added Tax Act, CAP 476
of the Laws of Kenya (hereinafter “VATA”);
(v) That the Respondent applied wrong VAT rates in raising VAT
assessments; and
(vi) That the Respondent confirmed the assessments without
due regard to all records/documents, explanations and
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 3 OF 33

information provided, thereby failing to appreciate all issues
presented and raised by the appellant before confirming the
assessment.
APPELLANT’S CASE
9. The Appellant case was premised on its statement of facts dated
and filed on 21st April, 2025
10. The Appellant stated that the Respondent conducted an audit
into the Appellant’s tax affairs for the years 2019 to 2023.The
Appellant stated that the Respondent obtained and analyzed the
its bank statements together with purchases claimed in order to
establish expected income, compared the same with the i-Tax
declarations, and subjected the variances to both Income Tax
and VAT, resulting in a total principal tax liability of Kshs.
17,752,866.00, comprising corporation Tax of Kshs.
1,858,132.00 and VAT of Kshs. 15,894,734.00, as reflected in
the table not reproduced.
11. The Appellant stated that it objected to the said assessments on
10th January 2025, clearly setting out the grounds of objection.
The Appellant stated that in disregard of the grounds of
objection and supporting documentation, the Respondent
confirmed the assessments through an Objection Decision dated
10th March 2025, which decision forms the subject of this Appeal.
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 4 OF 33

12. The Appellant stated that the Respondent erred in fact and in
law by charging Value Added Tax (VAT) on non-existent income
contrary to Section 5 of the VATA, having applied the bankings
approach to determine alleged undeclared income without
considering non-trade income items appearing in the bank
statements in a table not reproduced herein
13. The Appellant stated that the Respondent erred in fact and in
law by charging income tax on non-existent income contrary to
Section 3(1) and 3(2) of the ITA having relied on the bankings
approach while failing to consider non-trade income items
reflected in the bank statements.
14. The Appellant stated that the Respondent further erred in fact
and in law by failing to allow the whole expenditure incurred in
earning the income contrary to Section 15 of the ITA.
15. The Appellant stated that the Respondent erred in applying
incorrect VAT rates in raising the VAT assessments, whereas the
Appellant mainly deals in LPG whose VAT rate has varied
between zero-rated, eight percent, and sixteen percent, a fact
which ought to have been considered in the computations but
that however, no sales reconciliation was provided and
supporting VAT computations or invoices were submitted
16. The Appellant averred that mere statement of applicable rates is
insufficient.
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 5 OF 33

17. The Appellant stated that the Respondent’s act of confirming
the assessments without due consideration of all records,
documents, explanations, and information provided was unfair,
irrational, malicious, capricious, and contrary to the principles of
fair administrative action under Article 47 of the Constitution of
Kenya, 2010 (hereinafter “the Constitution”).
Appellants Prayers
18. The Appellant prayed that the Tribunal grants the following
orders:
(a) Allow the Appeal;
(b) Annuls the additional assessments aforesaid based on the
grounds above, as well as the Appellant's notice of
objection; and
(c) Awards costs of this appeal to the Appellant.
RESPONDENT’S CASE
19. In response to the appeal, the Respondent filed its Statement of
facts dated and filed on 29th July, 2025 wherein it stated that the
Appellant was reported by customers with claims of underfilling
gas cylinders and that a field operation was conducted at the
Appellant’s business on 16th November 2023.
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 6 OF 33

20. The Respondent stated that although the suspected consumer
fraud was not confirmed, analysis of the Appellant’s tax
compliance status revealed under-declarations of business
turnover and an intelligence summary was shared on 27th
August 2024. The Respondent stated that it served the
Appellant with a notice of intention to audit dated 16th October
2024.
21. The Respondent stated that it thereafter issued a notice of
findings and intention to issue additional assessments for the
period 2019 to 2023 on 4th December 2024. The Respondent
stated that additional assessments were subsequently issued on
16th December 2024.
22. The Respondent stated that the additional Income Tax
assessments for the years 2020, 2021, 2022 and 2023 were
based on variances between income tax returns filed and
income established under the banking test, and that the
variances were brought to charge.
23. The Respondent stated that it allowed 95 percent of the
turnover established as purchases based on petroleum sector
margins and further allowed 40 percent of gross profit as
operating expenses for the years 2020 and 2023 where Nil
returns had been filed.
24. The Respondent stated that it analyzed purchases declared in
Income Tax returns against purchases declared in VAT returns
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 7 OF 33

and noted variances. The Respondent stated that this analysis
culminated in the computation of additional taxable income and
tax due for the respective years.
25. The Respondent stated that VAT additional assessments for
December 2020, December 2021, December 2022 and April
2023 were based on variances between sales per the banking
test and sales declared in VAT returns, and on disallowed input
VAT claimed twice in October 2019.
26. The Respondent stated that the Appellant was notified of the
review and intention to issue additional assessments through a
letter dated 4th December 2024 and a demand notice dated 18th
December 2024, and that the assessments were raised on i-Tax
on 16th December 2024 for Income Tax and VAT totaling Kshs.
17,752,866.50 in principal tax.
27. The Respondent stated that the Appellant objected to the
assessments on 10th January 2025 on grounds that the
assessments were erroneous, irrational and excessive, that non-
existent income had been subjected to tax using the banking
test, that wrong VAT rates had been applied, and that input VAT
had been wrongly disallowed.
28. The Respondent stated that it requested supporting documents
including banking reconciliations, details of non-business
income, audited financial statements, sales and purchase
ledgers, VAT reconciliations, invoices, licenses, permits and
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 8 OF 33

bank statements, but that the Appellant failed to provide all the
requested documents.
29. The Respondent stated that consequently the Appellant failed to
discharge the burden of proof under Section 56(1) of the Tax
Procedures Act, CAP 469B of the Laws of Kenya (hereinafter
“TPA”). The Respondent stated that it reviewed the objection
and issued an objection decision dated 10th March 2025
confirming the assessments, and that the Appellant thereafter
lodged this Appeal.
30. The Respondent stated that the assessments were not
erroneous, irrational or excessive but were based on available
information and the Commissioner’s best judgment pursuant to
Section 31 of the TPA.
31. The Respondent stated that the income tax assessments were
properly based on the banking test and that allowances for
purchases and operating expenses were granted.
32. The Respondent stated that without adequate documentation, it
could not verify the Appellant’s claims of non-business income.
The Respondent stated that the disallowed input VAT related to
double claims and that the Appellant failed to provide invoices
and proof of payment.
33. The Respondent stated that the Appellant did not provide sales
reconciliations or licenses to support the claim of wrong VAT
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 9 OF 33

rates, and that the Appellant’s own returns declared general
rate sales.
34. The Respondent stated that the Appellant failed to discharge its
burden of proof as required under Section 56 of the TPA.
35. The Respondent stated that it relies on the audit notices,
findings, assessments, email correspondence, and objection
decision as documentary evidence. The Respondent stated that
all actions were taken in accordance with the TPA.
36. The Respondent stated that the additional assessments were
proper and lawful and that the Appellant failed to prove
otherwise.
37. The Respondent sought the following reliefs from the Tribunal:
(a) That the Tribunal upholds the objection decision dated 10th
March 2025 confirming Income Tax and VAT assessments;
and
(b) That the Tribunal dismisses the Appeal with costs to the
Respondent.
PARTIES’ WRITTEN SUBMISSIONS
38. By 4th November, 2025, the date of the hearing, The Appellant’s
submissions dated 15th December, 2025 and filed on even date
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 10 OF 33

even date are hereby adopted as are those of the Respondent
dated and filed on 17th December, 2025.
Appellant’s Submissions
39. In the Appellant’s submissions, it was stated that the
submissions were filed pursuant to Section 13 of the TATA and
the directions of the Tribunal.
40. The Appellant submitted that the appeal arises from the
Respondent’s objection decision dated 10th March 2025
confirming additional assessments for Income Tax (Company)
and Value Added Tax for the periods under review.
41. In the Appellant’s submissions, it was stated that the
Respondent’s assessments were founded almost entirely on a
banking analysis methodology applied mechanically, arbitrarily,
and without reconciliation of non-business receipts, applicable
VAT rates on LPG, allowable deductions, and genuine input VAT.
42. The Appellant submitted that the Respondent acted in excess of
statutory powers, misapplied the law, and violated the
Appellant’s constitutional right to fair administrative action.
43. In the Appellant’s submissions, it was stated that the issues for
determination included whether the additional assessments
were erroneous, irrational, and excessive; whether input VAT
was disallowed contrary to Section 17 of the VATA; whether the
assessments were arbitrary, irrational, applied incorrect VAT
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 11 OF 33

rates and were confirmed without regard to records,
explanations, and proportionality under Article 47 of the
Constitution; and whether the Appellant was entitled to the
reliefs sought.
44. The Appellant submitted that the additional assessments were
erroneous because they were based on bank statement
variances without proper reconciliation to actual business
operations, and that fixed turnover percentages used did not
reflect actual LPG business margins.
45. In the Appellant’s submissions, it was stated that the
Respondent assumed every bank credit was taxable turnover,
yet under Section 2 of the VATA chargeable only on a taxable
supply made in the course of business, and mere inflows without
linkage to a taxable transaction cannot constitute taxable
turnover, as observed in Republic v Kenya Revenue
Authority ex parte Jaffer Mohamed [2015] eKLR.
46. The Appellant submitted that the assessments were excessive
for failing to consider legitimate business expenses, turnover
fluctuations, and non-business deposits, thereby inflating tax
liability.
47. In the Appellant’s submissions, it was stated that Section 31(1)
of the TPA requires amendments to assessments to be
reasonable and based on identifiable business activity, and
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 12 OF 33

blanket assumptions without verification are inconsistent with
that provision.
48. The Appellant submitted that bank credits include non-revenue
transactions such as capital injections, loan proceeds, inter-
account transfers, contra entries, bounced cheque redeposits,
and refunds, which do not amount to taxable supplies, and that
once reconciliations and audited accounts were produced, the
burden shifted to the Commissioner under Section 56(1) of the
TPA.
49. In the Appellant’s submissions, it was stated that taxation must
rest strictly on the law and not on assumption, as held in Cape
Brandy Syndicate v IRC (1921) 1 KB 64, and that the
Respondent undertook no forensic analysis to distinguish
taxable receipts from non-revenue inflows, contrary to Section
29 of the TPA.
50. The Appellant submitted that the methodology applied lacked
rational basis, was speculative, and resulted in inflated tax
liabilities not corresponding with business reality and also
submitted that reconciliations, audited accounts, and
explanations demonstrated that the additional assessments
were arbitrary, excessive, and unsupported by verifiable
business data.
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 13 OF 33

51. The Appellant submitted that Section 17(1) of the VATA entitles
deduction of input VAT on goods and services used in making
taxable supplies and that disallowances were applied generally
and indiscriminately, affecting legitimate LPG purchases and
that invoices, delivery notes, and proof of payment
demonstrated authenticity and legitimacy of transactions and
contradicted claims of duplication.
52. The Appellant submitted that disallowance of input VAT must be
based on clear and verifiable evidence and not suspicion or
clerical errors and that the disallowance disproportionately
penalized the taxpayer and failed to reflect actual business
activity.
53. The Appellant submitted that the Respondent did not seek
clarification or engage with the Appellant before disallowing
input VAT, undermining procedural fairness and that the
Respondent overlooked normal LPG industry practices involving
multiple deliveries generating sequential invoices.
54. The Appellant submitted that blanket disallowance without
verification infringed statutory rights and procedural standards
and the Tribunal was encouraged to adjust VAT assessments
and allow legitimate input VAT in compliance with Section 17 of
the VATA.
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 14 OF 33

55. The Appellant submitted that additional assessments were
arbitrary and irrational for being based on unreconciled bank
variances, fixed turnover percentages, and incorrect VAT rate
application without distinguishing standard-rated, exempt, or
other supplies.
56. In the Appellant’s submissions, it was stated that ignoring
reconciliations, invoices, and documentation led to
misapplication of VAT rates and assessments inconsistent with
proportionality and fairness under Article 47 of the Constitution.
57. The Appellant submitted that assessments were excessive for
ignoring expenses, turnover fluctuations, and proper VAT
classification, and that treating all bank credits as taxable
income inflated liability.
58. In the Appellant’s submissions, it was stated that Section 31(1)
of the TPA requires reasonable amendments based on
identifiable activity and procedural fairness requires
engagement with taxpayer evidence.
59. The Appellant submitted that once reconciliations and audited
accounts explaining deposits were produced, the evidential
burden shifted to the Commissioner under Section 56(1) of the
TPA, and failure to engage rendered the approach arbitrary and
that the Respondent ignored correct VAT rate distinctions and
applied a general VAT rate across products.
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 15 OF 33

60. The Appellant submitted that assessments imposed outside
empowering provisions, ignoring evidence or based on irrational
assumptions, cannot withstand scrutiny and that Section 29 of
the TPA requires reliance on available information, not
speculation.
61. The Appellant submitted that assessments were arbitrary,
irrational, unlawful, founded on unverified bank credits, ignored
reconciliations, misapplied VAT rates, and failed to consider
documentation, and should be set aside with costs awarded.
62. The Appellant submitted that the assessment disregarded
statutory command and evidence, equated every bank credit to
turnover, and that once credible reconciliations and
documentation were produced, the Respondent failed to justify
treating deposits as taxable supplies under Section 56(1) of the
TPA.
63. In the Appellant’s submissions, it was stated that the
assessment contravened the ITA, VATA and TPA, and the
objection decision should be set aside, taxes vacated, and costs
awarded.
64. The Appellant submitted that the Respondent erred in law and
fact by treating all bank credits as taxable turnover, applying
incorrect VAT rates, failing to verify documentation, and
breaching Articles 47 and 50 of the Constitution, Section 17 of
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 16 OF 33

the VATA and Sections 29, 31, and 56 of the TPA, rendering the
assessments arbitrary, excessive, and unlawful.
Respondent’s Submissions
65. In its submissions, the Respondent opposed the Appeal as
contained in the Appellant’s Memorandum of Appeal and
Statement of Facts and relied on its Statement of Facts dated
29th July 2025 together with all annexures thereto and the
submissions filed and contended that the issues for
determination before the Tribunal were whether the Respondent
was justified in raising additional corporate income tax and VAT
assessments and whether the Appellant discharged its burden
of proof.
66. The Respondent submitted that the additional income tax
assessments for the years 2020, 2021, 2022 and 2023 were
issued based on variances between the income tax returns filed
and income established through the banking test, and that the
variance was brought to charge for Income Tax purposes. The
Respondent allowed 95% of the turnover established as
purchases based on margins for petroleum sector dealers and
further allowed 40% of the gross profit as operating expenses in
the years 2020 and 2023 where Nil returns had been filed.
67. In the Respondent submissions, it was stated that the VAT
additional assessments for December 2020, December 2021,
December 2022 and April 2023 were issued based on variances
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 17 OF 33

between sales as per the banking test and sales declared in VAT
returns, and that input tax claimed in October 2019 was
disallowed on grounds that it had been claimed twice.
68. The Respondent submitted that the Appellant was requested to
provide detailed banking reconciliations, supporting
documentation for non-business income, audited financial
statements, detailed sales and purchase ledgers, VAT sales
reconciliations, copies of disallowed purchase invoices and proof
of payment, EPRA license, single business permits and bank
statements, but failed to provide the same.
69. The Respondent relied on Section 31 of the TPA, which
empowers the Commissioner to amend an assessment based on
available information and to the best of the Commissioner’s
judgment to ensure that the taxpayer is liable for the correct
amount of tax payable
70. The Respondent further relied on Section 59 of the TPA, which
grants the Commissioner authority to require production of
documents, furnishing of information, and attendance for
purposes of obtaining full information in respect of tax liability.
71. The Respondent submitted that the burden of keeping and
producing records lies with the taxpayer and cited the decision
in Republic v Kenya Revenue Authority; Proto Energy
Limited (Exparte) (Judicial Review Application E023 of
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 18 OF 33

2021) [2022], where the Court held that a presumption of
correctness attaches to the Commissioner’s assessment and
remains until the taxpayer produces competent and relevant
evidence to support its position.
72. In the Respondent submissions, it was contended that the
Appellant failed to discharge its burden of proof under Section
56(1) of the TPA and Section 30 of the TATA, having failed to
provide the requested documentation.
73. The Respondent relied on TAT No. 28 of 2018 – Boycott
General Contractors Limited v Kenya Revenue Authority,
where the Tribunal held that the taxpayer bears the burden of
proving that an assessment is wrong and cannot rely on
sweeping allegations without substantial support.
74. The Respondent further relied on Commissioner of
Investigations & Enforcements v Dr. Evans Kidero (2022)
eKLR, where the High Court emphasized the taxpayer’s duty to
keep records within the self-assessment regime and to provide
sufficient evidence to discharge the burden of proof.
75. Reliance was also placed on Osho Drapers Limited v
Commissioner of Domestic Taxes [2022] eKLR, where the
Court held that a taxpayer must produce documents to
discharge its burden under Section 56(1) of the TPA.
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 19 OF 33

76. Additionally, reference was made to a High Court decision
delivered on 18th March 2022 by A. Mabeya J, where the Court
held that it was incumbent upon the Appellant to establish a
proper trail of payments and produce documentary evidence of
alleged purchases, failing to which the appeal lacked merit and
was dismissed with costs.
ISSUES FOR DETERMINATION
77. The Tribunal having carefully evaluated parties’ pleadings it is of
the respectful view that the single issue that call for its
determination is as hereunder:
Whether the Appellant discharged the burden of proving
that the Objection Decision dated 10th March 2025 was
incorrect.
ANALYSIS AND FINDINGS
78. Having identified this issue for determination, the Tribunal will
proceed to analyse the same as hereinunder:
Whether the Appellant discharged the burden of proving
that the Objection Decision dated 10th March 2025 was
incorrect.
79. The Tribunal have reviewed the context of the dispute that
originated from customer complaints regarding underfilling of
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 20 OF 33

LPG cylinders, prompting the Respondent to conduct a field
operation on 16th November 2023, which did not confirm fraud
but triggered a compliance review that revealed under
declaration of turnover that culminated in an intelligence report
issued on 27th August 2024.
80. Subsequently, the Respondent issued a notice of intention to
audit on 16th October 2024, followed by a notice of findings and
intention to assess on 4th December 2024, and thereafter raised
additional income tax and VAT assessments on 16th December
2024 for the period 2019 to 2023 amounting to Kshs.
17,752,866.00.
81. The Appellant objected to the assessments on 10th January
2025, challenging their legality, methodology, and accuracy.
However, the Respondent requested for further documentation,
and thereafter issued an objection decision on 10th March 2025
confirming the assessments. Dissatisfied, the Appellant filed a
Notice of Appeal on 7th April 2025 and subsequently lodged the
Memorandum of Appeal and Statement of Facts on 21st April
2025, leading to the present proceedings.
82. The Tribunal notes several inconsistencies, that the
Respondent’s intervention was triggered by customer
complaints alleging underfilling of LPG cylinders, and indeed a
field operation was conducted to verify this claim. However, the
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 21 OF 33

Respondent expressly concedes that the suspected consumer
fraud was not confirmed.
83. The Tribunal notes that the Respondent proceeded to undertake
a tax compliance review and ultimately raised substantial tax
assessments based on alleged under-declaration of income
which creates a logical disconnect, the initial allegation that
justified the investigation was unproven, but it formed the
springboard for a far-reaching tax assessment grounded on
entirely different considerations.
84. The Tribunal also notes that in the application of the banking
test as a proxy for taxable income, the Respondent treated
variances between bank deposits and declared income as
undisclosed turnover, effectively presuming that all inflows into
the Appellant’s bank accounts constituted taxable business
receipts however the Appellant maintains that the bank
statements contained a mixture of business and non-business
inflows, including loans, capital injections, inter-account
transfers, and other non-revenue items, The inconsistency here
is methodological, while the Respondent adopts a blanket
approach equating bank credits to taxable income. The
Appellant challenged this as a characterization of financial
reality and inferred that without a clear reconciliation
distinguishing taxable from non-taxable inflows, the reliability of
the banking test as applied becomes questionable.
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 22 OF 33

85. In regards to the treatment of business expenses and
deductions, the Tribunal notes the Respondent’s assertion that
it allowed 95% of turnover as purchases and 40% of gross profit
as operating expenses based on sectoral margins, particularly in
years where nil returns were filed. However, the Appellant
contends that the genuine expenses actually incurred in the
production of income were not fully considered or allowed,
contrary to statutory provisions.
86. This reveals a tension between estimated allowances derived
from industry averages and the Appellant’s claim of actual
expenditure supported by records. The inconsistency lies in
substituting real financial data with generalized assumptions,
which may not accurately reflect the Appellant’s specific
business circumstances.
87. Further in the disallowance of input VAT. The Tribunal notes
that the Respondent justified the disallowance on grounds of
duplication of claims and lack of supporting documentation,
whereas the Appellant insists that the input VAT related to
legitimate transactions supported by invoices, delivery notes,
and proof of payment which is a factual contradiction as to
whether the documentation existed and whether it was duly
considered. The Tribunal is of the view that if such
documentation was indeed available but disregarded, it would
suggest procedural unfairness; if it was not provided, then the
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 23 OF 33

Respondent’s position gains strength. The Tribunal has
therefore evaluated the evidence against the adequacy of
document verification.
88. The Tribunal notes that the application of VAT rates also
presented a technical inconsistency. The Appellant argued that
LPG products have been subject to varying VAT treatments over
time, zero-rated, 8%, and 16%, to which the Respondent failed
to account for these variations in computing VAT liabilities. On
the other hand, the Respondent maintains that the Appellant
declared general rated sales and failed to substantiate the
application of alternative rates. This inconsistency reflects a
disagreement not just on facts but on the proper classification of
supplies and the applicable tax regime, which directly affects
the accuracy of the VAT assessments.
89. With regard to the burden of proof and the production of
supporting documentation, the Tribunal notes the Respondent’s
repeated assertion that the Appellant failed to provide critical
documents such as reconciliations, audited accounts, and
transaction records, thereby failing to discharge the statutory
burden of proof. Conversely, the Appellant claimed that
reconciliations and supporting documents were indeed provided
but were ignored or inadequately considered. This calls for the
Tribunal’s perusal of the conduct of the objection process,
specifically whether the Respondent fairly evaluated the
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 24 OF 33

material placed before it or prematurely dismissed the
Appellant’s explanations.
90. The Tribunal finds that collectively, these inconsistencies point
to both methodological weaknesses and procedural tensions in
the Respondent’s assessment process, as well as factual
disputes that go to the heart of whether the additional tax
liabilities were properly and lawfully determined and the
Appellant discharged the burden of proof under Section 56(1) of
the TPA.
91. In determining this issue, the Tribunal acknowledged that the
Respondent is statutorily empowered under the TPA to raise
additional assessments using available information and to apply
a “best judgment” approach where a taxpayer’s records are
inadequate or unreliable. Under Section 31, of the TPA the
Commissioner is permitted to apply best judgment where
records are incomplete and the Tribunal finds that in the instant
appeal, the Respondent acted within the law. The assessments
cannot be termed erroneous or excessive where they are
derived from available financial data.
92. In the present case, the Respondent relied on bank statements,
purchases declared, and i-Tax filings to identify variances, and
thereafter applied the banking test to reconstruct the
Appellant’s income. The Tribunal finds that, prima facie, the use
of the banking test is a permissible audit tool, particularly where
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 25 OF 33

discrepancies exist between declared income and actual
financial flows.
93. However, the Tribunal interrogated whether the manner in
which the banking test was applied was reasonable,
proportionate, and grounded in verifiable business activity. The
Appellant’s contention that the Respondent treated all bank
credits as taxable income without distinguishing non-business
inflows raises a significant concern. A proper application of the
banking test requires a reconciliation exercise to isolate taxable
receipts from non-taxable items such as loans, capital injections,
or inter-account transfers. The absence of such differentiation
would render the methodology overly rigid and potentially
arbitrary. However, the Respondent requested on 15th January
2025, the following documents to inform its review:
 A detailed banking reconciliation for the period under
review together with the relevant supporting
documents.
 Composition of the non-business income together with
the relevant supporting documentation.
 The audited financial statements for the period under
review.
 Detailed sales and purchases ledgers for the period
under review.
 Detailed sales reconciliations indicating the VAT
charged in the period under
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 26 OF 33

 Copies of the purchase invoices disallowed and
evidence of payment for the purchases.
 EPRA license and single business permit.
 Copy of the bank statements for the period under
review.
 Copies of the bank statement.
94. The Tribunal notes that the Appellant was only able to provide
the following documents:
 A Letter stating clearly the grounds of objection.
 A breakdown of changes of VAT rates for LPG the
Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) over the period under
review.
95. Where a taxpayer fails to provide records, the Commissioner is
justified in reconstructing income using indirect methods such
as the banking test under Section 31 of the TPA. The finding of
the Tribunal is that the Respondent was justified, as the
Appellant did not discharge the evidentiary burden.
96. The Tribunal further considers whether the Respondent’s
reliance on estimated sectoral margins namely the allowance of
95% purchases and 40% operating expenses was justified.
While estimation is permissible under best judgment
assessments, it must still bear a rational nexus to the taxpayer’s
actual business operations. Application of estimates
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 27 OF 33

mechanistically without reference to the Appellant’s specific
records or industry realities, weakens the probative value of the
assessment.
97. On VAT, the Tribunal examined whether the Respondent
correctly linked bank deposits to taxable supplies and whether
the applicable VAT rates were properly applied. Given the
Appellant’s argument that LPG attracts varying VAT treatments,
failure to distinguish between zero-rated, exempt, and standard-
rated supplies points to a flawed computation. Similarly, the
disallowance of input VAT must be supported by clear evidence
of duplication or lack of documentation; otherwise, it risks being
viewed as arbitrary.
98. Ultimately, the Tribunal finds that while the Respondent was
entitled to initiate the assessments and rely on the banking test,
the validity of the assessment’s hinged on the quality and rigor
of the analysis applied. Where the Respondent failed to
demonstrate that the assessments were based on a reasonable
and properly verified reconstruction of income, the justification
for confirming the assessments becomes questionable.
99. The Tribunal notes that Section 31(1) of the TPA provides that
the Commissioner may amend an assessment… from the
available information and to the best of the Commissioner’s
judgment… to ensure that the taxpayer is liable for the correct
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 28 OF 33

amount of tax payable. This provision empowers the
Commissioner to rely on available information, including bank
statements, where proper records are not provided.
100. On this issue, the Tribunal is guided by Section 56(1) of the
TPA, which places the burden squarely on the taxpayer to prove
that a tax decision is incorrect. The Respondent’s assessments
are presumed correct unless displaced by credible and cogent
evidence. Accordingly, the Appellant is required to provide
sufficient documentation and reconciliations to demonstrate
that the bank deposits included non-taxable inflows, that
expenses claimed were legitimate, and that input VAT
deductions were properly supported. Section 17 of the VATA
provides that, Input VAT is only deductible were supported by
valid tax invoices and proof of payment. The Tribunal finds that
this was not supported
101. The Tribunal notes that the Appellant advanced substantive
arguments challenging the methodology used, including the
improper application of the banking test, failure to consider non-
business income, incorrect VAT rates, and unlawful disallowance
of input VAT. While these arguments raise serious legal and
factual questions, the Tribunal assessed whether they were
backed by documentary evidence such as audited accounts,
bank reconciliations, invoices, and tax computations. Mere
assertions, however persuasive, are insufficient to discharge the
statutory burden.
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 29 OF 33

102. The Respondent maintained that the Appellant failed to
provide key documents despite being requested to do so,
including reconciliations, ledgers, invoices, and proof of
payment. The Tribunal finds that such documentation was
indeed not furnished, or was incomplete, and concludes that the
Appellant failed to discharge the burden of proof. In such
circumstances, the Respondent is entitled to rely on its best
judgment assessment, even if imperfect, as long as it is not
shown to be wholly unreasonable.
103. The Tribunal is guided by the case of Silver Chain Limited V
Commissioner Income Tax & 3 Others [2016] eKLR where
the court held as follows:
‘….From the pleadings, herein, I am satisfied that the
applicant was not given an opportunity to explain his position
on the sales reports or the entire operations of the company.
What is being demanded as tax is what was unilaterally
computed by the respondents. Although the respondents are
empowered by the law to assess what a tax payer ought to
pay, it is prudent that while undertaking such an exercise, the
tax payer be given an opportunity to explain its position. The
volumes of sales do not dictate the profit margins. It all
depend on the type of business. The applicant is running a
restaurant. If one only computes the sales volume without
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 30 OF 33

knowing the costs of those sales as well as other outgoings
such as water, electricity and garbage collection bills, then the
amount of tax demanded may not reflect the truth. ….
104. The Tribunal also cites the case of Digital Box Limited vs
Commissioner of Investigation & Enforcement [Tax
Appeal No 115 of 2017] where it held as follows:
“The Tribunal tasked with a similar matter held that the
taxpayer had failed to specify the entries in the bank
statement which should not have been included in the
assessment and the reasons why they ought not to have been
included. The Tribunal further found that Digital Box Limited
had failed to discharge its burden of proving that KRA had
misapplied the bank analysis test. The Respondent argued
that the judgement of the Tribunal reemphasizes the position
that all bank deposits are subject to taxation, and it is for the
account holder to explain why the funds should not be taxed.
……..”
105. The Tribunal found that the Respondent acted within its legal
mandate to determine the Appellant’s tax liability using its best
judgment, including the reliance it placed on banking analysis
and applicable industry margins where necessary. In the
absence of credible reconciliations or supporting documentation
from the Appellant, this approach was deemed reasonable,
logical, and sufficient. The Tribunal further noted that although
the burden could have shifted back to the Respondent had the
JUDGEMENT- TAT APPEAL NO. E376 OF 2025 – SMARTGAS ACCESSORIES COMPANY LIMITED VS COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES AND BOARD COORDINATION
PAGE 31 OF 33

Appellant provided verifiable evidence that was ignored, no
such circumstance arose in this case.
106. Accordingly, the Respondent was justified in issuing and
confirming the additional assessments under Section 31 of the
TPA based on available information. The Appellant failed to
discharge its burden of proof under Section 56(1) of the TPA by
not providing essential documents such as bank reconciliations,
audited financial statements, and sales and purchase records.
Additionally, the input VAT claims did not meet the
requirements under Section 17 of the VATA. Consequently, the
additional income tax and VAT assessments for the period 2019
to 2023 were lawfully issued, properly confirmed, and grounded
in statutory authority, leading the Tribunal to uphold the
Respondent’s decision and disallow the objection.
FINAL DECISION
107. The upshot to the foregoing is that the Tribunal finds and holds
that the Appeal fails and makes the following Orders:
(a) The Appeal be and is hereby dismissed.
(b) The Respondent’s Objection Decision on 10th March 2025
be and is hereby upheld
(c) Each party to bear its own cost.
108. It is so Ordered.
